content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom needs to ___ his teeth checked by the dentist before he travels abroad next month.
distractor_rationale: 'do his teeth checked' is grammatically incorrect; 'do' cannot introduce a causative structure with object + past participle
'make his teeth checked' is incorrect; 'make' requires an infinitive without 'to', not a past participle, in causative structures
'get done his teeth checked' is a double-marking error that creates a redundant and ungrammatical string

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria's laptop was very slow, so she ___ it repaired at the local shop last week.
distractor_rationale: 'got it to repair' inserts an incorrect infinitive marker; the causative requires object + bare past participle, not 'to' + infinitive
'had it to repair' also incorrectly uses 'to' + infinitive after the object instead of the required past participle
'made' requires a bare infinitive after the object, not a past participle, so 'made it repaired' is ungrammatical

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The windows in our office look very dirty — we really should ___ them cleaned before the clients arrive on Friday.
distractor_rationale: 'get them to clean' changes the meaning entirely — it means asking the windows themselves to clean, which is nonsensical; the causative requires 'get + object + past participle'
'have them to clean' incorrectly inserts 'to' before the base form; causative 'have' requires object + bare past participle
'be them cleaned' is ungrammatical; 'be' cannot introduce a causative structure with an intervening object

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: After the storm, several trees in the garden had fallen over, so the family had to ___ them removed by a professional team.
distractor_rationale: 'get them to remove' shifts meaning to instructing the trees to act, which is absurd; the causative requires 'get + object + past participle', not 'to' + infinitive
'make them removed' is ungrammatical; 'make' in causative structures requires a bare infinitive, not a past participle
'do them removed' is entirely ungrammatical; 'do' cannot function as a causative verb in this structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James was cycling home when he ___ hit by a car that ran a red light at the junction.
distractor_rationale: 'had hit by a car' is ungrammatical in the causative sense here because James did not arrange to be hit; 'had' causative implies deliberate arrangement, which is contextually impossible
'was being hit' describes an ongoing action in the past, but the event is a sudden, completed incident, making past continuous semantically wrong
'got himself hit' implies deliberate self-inflicted action, which contradicts the accidental context established by 'ran a red light'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara decided to ___ her living room repainted in a lighter colour because the old walls made the space feel dark.
distractors: get her living room to repaint
have her living room to repaint
distractor_rationale: 'get her living room to repaint' uses 'to' + infinitive after the object, which incorrectly implies the living room will do the repainting itself
'have her living room to repaint' also inserts 'to' before the base form, violating the causative rule that requires object + bare past participle
'make her living room repainted' is ungrammatical; 'make' in causative structures requires object + bare infinitive, not a past participle

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The chef's new recipe ___ praised by all the food critics who attended the launch event at the restaurant.
distractor_rationale: 'had praised by all the food critics' is ungrammatical; 'have' in the causative requires an object between 'have' and the past participle, and this sentence's subject cannot deliberately arrange its own praise in this construction
'got itself praised' adds a reflexive pronoun that is unnatural and ungrammatical in informal passive; 'get + V3' does not take a reflexive object
'was getting praised' describes an ongoing or repeated process, but the context specifies a single completed event at a specific launch, making past continuous semantically incorrect

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Before the annual inspection, the manager arranged to ___ the fire safety equipment tested by a certified engineer, as required by company policy.
distractors: get the fire safety equipment to test
have the fire safety equipment to be tested
make the fire safety equipment tested
distractor_rationale: 'get the fire safety equipment to test' uses 'to' + infinitive, implying the equipment will perform the testing itself, which is semantically absurd and structurally wrong for causative 'get'
'have the fire safety equipment to be tested' incorrectly combines 'to be' with the past participle, creating a non-standard and ungrammatical hybrid of passive infinitive and causative
'make the fire safety equipment tested' is ungrammatical; 'make' in causative structures requires a bare infinitive after the object, not a past participle

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Although Anna had prepared the report herself, she still wanted to ___ it proofread by a colleague before submitting it to the board.
distractor_rationale: 'have it to proofread' incorrectly inserts 'to' before the base verb; causative 'have' requires object + bare past participle, not 'to' + infinitive
'get it to proofread' uses 'to' + infinitive, implying the report will proofread something else, which is semantically impossible and structurally incorrect for causative 'get'
'make it proofread' is ungrammatical; 'make' in a causative structure requires a bare infinitive after the object, and 'proofread' here would be misread as base form with wrong meaning

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David's phone ___ stolen while he was waiting at the busy train station, so he immediately called his bank to cancel his cards.
distractor_rationale: 'had stolen' implies David arranged for someone to steal his phone, which directly contradicts the accidental, victimised context described in the sentence
'was being stolen' describes an ongoing process, but the theft is presented as a completed event that prompted an immediate response, making past continuous semantically inconsistent

'got it stolen' inserts a redundant object pronoun; in the informal passive 'get + V3', the subject itself undergoes the action and no additional object pronoun is inserted between 'got' and the past participle

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My phone screen was cracked, so I had it ___ at the repair shop near my office.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after causative 'have + object' is a common learner error; the correct form is bare past participle
present participle produces an active meaning ('it was replacing'), which is grammatically wrong in this causative structure
bare infinitive is used in causative 'make/let + object + infinitive', not in 'have + object + past participle'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom got ___ in a minor accident on his way home, but fortunately he was not injured.
distractor_rationale: bare infinitive after 'got' in the informal passive is incorrect; the past participle is required
to-infinitive after 'get' signals a purpose or intention, not an informal passive meaning
present participle after 'got' would suggest an active, ongoing action rather than a passive event that happened to him

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Before the annual inspection, the manager asked all staff to get the office equipment ___.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after 'get + object' creates the wrong structure; learners often confuse this with 'get someone to do something', but here the object is a thing, requiring the past participle
present participle after 'get + object' implies the equipment is actively doing the checking, which is semantically absurd
'be checked' belongs to the formal passive construction 'be + past participle', not the informal causative 'get + object + past participle'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara decided to get her apartment ___ by a professional painter before she moved in, as the walls were in poor condition.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after 'get + object' is a classic learner error that confuses causative 'get + object + V3' with the structure 'get someone to do something'
present participle after 'get + object' implies Sara's apartment was actively doing the repainting, which is impossible and semantically wrong
bare infinitive is not used after 'get + object' in the causative; it is the form required after 'make + object' or 'let + object', not 'get + object'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Although David had planned to fix the boiler himself, he eventually had it ___ by a certified engineer after realising the fault was more serious than he had thought.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after 'have + object' is incorrect in the causative structure; this error arises from confusion with 'get someone to do something', where 'to' is required before a person, not a thing

bare infinitive after 'have + object' is the pattern for 'make + object + infinitive', not the causative 'have + object + past participle', where the past participle signals that the action is done to the object
present participle after 'have + object' would imply the boiler was actively repairing something else, which contradicts the sentence's meaning that David arranged for it to be fixed

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria needs to get her eyes ___ before she can order new glasses.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive cannot follow get in the causative structure; the correct form is bare past participle
present participle -ing is used after get only when describing the subject actively doing something, not arranging for it to be done
bare infinitive without to cannot complete the causative get + object + V3 structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom's laptop stopped working, so he got it ___ at the repair shop near his office.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive is the most common learner error in causative get; the object must be followed by a past participle, not to-infinitive
-ing form after the object signals the object is actively doing the action itself, which is impossible for a laptop
bare infinitive is used after causative make or let, not after causative get

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The kitchen looked much better after Anna had the walls ___.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after have + object signals the object will do the action itself, which walls cannot do
-ing after have + object means the object is actively performing the action, which is impossible for walls
bare infinitive cannot follow have + object in the causative structure; a past participle is required

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: James was ___ when he slipped on the wet floor, so he had to leave work early.
distractor_rationale: -ing form here would mean James was actively hurting someone else, which contradicts the context of him leaving work
to-infinitive cannot follow the verb be in this passive or result-state structure
hurt is an irregular verb; 'hurted' is a common learner error applying regular past participle rules to an irregular form

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We couldn't move into the apartment until we got the locks ___.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after get + object is a very frequent learner error; the causative requires a past participle
bare infinitive is used after causative make or let, not after causative get + object
-ing after get + object implies the object is actively performing the action, but locks cannot change themselves

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara usually gets her hair ___ every six weeks at the salon downtown.
distractor_rationale: -ing after get + object implies Sara's hair is actively cutting something, which is nonsensical
to-infinitive is the most common error learners make in causative get; the correct form is past participle
third-person singular present tense 'cuts' cannot follow get + object in the causative construction

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: David had his annual report ___ by a professional editor before submitting it to the board.
distractor_rationale: to-infinitive after have + object incorrectly signals that the report itself will proofread something
-ing after have + object implies the report is actively proofreading, which is impossible
third-person singular present form cannot follow have + object in the causative structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The project manager got the budget figures ___ twice before presenting them to the client.
distractor_rationale: 'being checked' is a progressive passive form used in formal written registers, not in the informal causative get + object + V3 construction
'to be checked' is a formal passive infinitive used after verbs like need or want, not after causative get + object
bare infinitive 'check' is used after causative make or let, not after causative get + object

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa's phone ___ stolen while she was waiting for the bus, so she had to borrow one to call home.
distractor_rationale: 'was being stolen' describes an action in progress at a past moment, which contradicts the sudden completed event implied by the context
'has been stolen' uses present perfect, which conflicts with the clearly past time frame established by 'while she was waiting'
'had stolen' makes Lisa the active subject who stole something herself, which is the opposite of the intended meaning

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Rather than fixing the heating system herself, she decided to get it ___ by a qualified engineer before winter arrived.
distractor_rationale: 'to be inspected' is a formal passive infinitive appropriate after verbs such as need or want, but it cannot follow get + object in the causative structure
'being inspected' is a formal progressive passive form used in written or official contexts, not in the informal causative get + object + V3 pattern
bare infinitive 'inspect' is used after causative make or let, not after causative get + object

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria needs to get her eyes to tested before she buys new glasses.
content.error_sentence: Maria needs to get her eyes to tested before she buys new glasses.
content.corrected_sentence: Maria needs to get her eyes tested before she buys new glasses.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom got his hair to cut at the new salon on the high street last Saturday.
content.error_sentence: Tom got his hair to cut at the new salon on the high street last Saturday.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom got his hair cut at the new salon on the high street last Saturday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We had the kitchen to repaint last month because the walls were badly stained.
content.error_sentence: We had the kitchen to repaint last month because the walls were badly stained.
content.corrected_sentence: We had the kitchen repainted last month because the walls were badly stained.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara got very surprising when she found out that the meeting had been cancelled.
content.error_sentence: Sara got very surprising when she found out that the meeting had been cancelled.
content.corrected_sentence: Sara got very surprised when she found out that the meeting had been cancelled.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: James decided to have his old laptop to repair rather than buy a brand-new one.
content.error_sentence: James decided to have his old laptop to repair rather than buy a brand-new one.
content.corrected_sentence: James decided to have his old laptop repaired rather than buy a brand-new one.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The report was got submitted by the team just before the deadline on Friday afternoon.
content.error_sentence: The report was got submitted by the team just before the deadline on Friday afternoon.
content.corrected_sentence: The report was submitted by the team just before the deadline on Friday afternoon.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna got the travel documents checked herself at the embassy, so she didn't need any help from an agent.
content.error_sentence: Anna got the travel documents checked herself at the embassy, so she didn't need any help from an agent.
content.corrected_sentence: Anna had the travel documents checked at the embassy, so she didn't need any help from an agent.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: After the storm, David got the roof inspecting by a professional builder to check for any serious damage.
content.error_sentence: After the storm, David got the roof inspecting by a professional builder to check for any serious damage.
content.corrected_sentence: After the storm, David got the roof inspected by a professional builder to check for any serious damage.
